summary.interpretation: Non-quic-go evidence is broad enough to reject an implementation-absence explanation, but each stack has a different claim boundary.

implementations.name: Cloudflare quiche
implementations.usage_reason: Widely known QUIC implementation; strong path event API
implementations.next_action: Use as cross-implementation client/server migration evidence
implementations.risk_note: Library/sample evidence is strong, but Cloudflare managed edge behavior is a separate deployment claim.

implementations.name: AWS s2n-quic
implementations.usage_reason: Best AWS NLB story because CID provider can be customized
implementations.next_action: Custom AWS NLB CID provider proof restored and rerun; live AWS NLB+s2n target test remains follow-up
implementations.risk_note: Library tests are positive; live AWS NLB forwarding and active source migration remain separate phases.

implementations.name: ngtcp2
implementations.usage_reason: C library and nghttp3 ecosystem with documented migration APIs
implementations.next_action: Use as C library primitive/path-validation comparison plus official osslclient/osslserver local HTTP/3 runtime positive control
implementations.risk_note: Official osslclient/osslserver local HTTP/3 runtime row is positive; browser handover and managed deployment remain separate claims.

implementations.name: LiteSpeed lsquic
implementations.usage_reason: High HTTP/3 deployment relevance with full unit suite plus preferred-address and NAT rebinding app demos
implementations.next_action: Use as preferred-address and NAT-rebinding app-level positive control; OpenLiteSpeed production-like demo remains follow-up
implementations.risk_note: Example app demos are positive; OpenLiteSpeed production-like deployment is still follow-up.

implementations.name: MsQuic
implementations.usage_reason: Microsoft ecosystem; mature deployment docs and rebind/path-validation tests
implementations.next_action: Use as production-relevant NAT rebinding/path-validation evidence; API audit shows constrained local-address control rather than quic-go-style AddPath/Probe/Switch
implementations.risk_note: NAT rebind/path validation tests are positive; API audit shows constrained local-address control, while QUIC-aware load balancing remains a deployment boundary.

implementations.name: Quinn
implementations.risk_note: Use as implementation maturity evidence, not as browser or managed-deployment proof.

implementations.name: Neqo
implementations.next_action: Use as Firefox-adjacent transport maturity evidence; Firefox browser runtime proof remains a separate gate
implementations.risk_note: Use as implementation maturity evidence, not as browser or managed-deployment proof.

implementations.name: XQUIC
implementations.usage_reason: Alibaba implementation; NAT rebinding demo verified
implementations.next_action: Use as NAT rebinding demo evidence; Linux full-suite replay runner is packaged in harness/scripts/run-xquic-full-suite-linux.sh because macOS AppleClang Werror blocks QPACK unit build
implementations.risk_note: NAT rebinding demo passed and Linux full-suite replay runner is packaged; full-suite PASS still requires a Linux ok artifact.

implementations.name: Chromium Chrome Cronet
implementations.usage_reason: Dominant browser client; Android experiment target
implementations.next_action: Use Chromium/Cronet policy-boundary audit as high-usage client evidence; runtime Chrome/Cronet handover still requires active network-change rows
implementations.risk_note: Policy hooks, NetLog migration events, and Cronet default-disable evidence exist, but browser handover success still requires runtime rows.

implementations.name: AWS CloudFront
implementations.next_action: Use CDN edge boundary audit for viewer-edge versus origin-end-to-end separation; live viewer-edge experiment remains follow-up
implementations.risk_note: Official docs support viewer-edge HTTP/3 Connection Migration, but origin end-to-end QUIC CM is not established.

implementations.name: AWS NLB plus s2n-quic
implementations.usage_reason: AWS passthrough QUIC with CID routing
implementations.next_action: s2n custom CID local provider proof PASS; next run live AWS NLB target A/B forwarding and migration continuity
implementations.risk_note: Local CID provider proof exists, but live target forwarding and active migration are pending.

implementations.name: mvfst
implementations.usage_reason: Meta scale transport with mature path manager and migration tests
implementations.next_action: Use source audit plus packaged focused Linux runner as large-scale implementation maturity evidence; Buck/getdeps execution remains follow-up
implementations.risk_note: Focused source/test map is strong and a Linux focused-test runner is packaged; local build/test execution remains gated by Buck/getdeps/disk.

implementations.name: picoquic
implementations.usage_reason: Research and interop implementation with rich migration edge-case tests
implementations.next_action: Use as edge-case maturity and preferred-address comparison
implementations.risk_note: Use as implementation maturity evidence, not as browser or managed-deployment proof.

implementations.name: nginx QUIC
implementations.usage_reason: Open-source web server implementation with serious server-side migration handling
implementations.next_action: Use as server-side runtime active-client-migration positive control; browser handover, Linux quic_bpf, and production deployment remain follow-up
implementations.risk_note: Server runtime evidence is positive; Linux quic_bpf and browser handover are separate claims.

implementations.name: quicly
implementations.usage_reason: H2O modular C implementation with path validation and promotion internals
implementations.next_action: Use as focused e2e path-migration evidence plus packaged Linux full-e2e runner; full t/e2e.t PASS still requires validation=ok_full_e2e
implementations.risk_note: Focused path-migration e2e is positive and a Linux full-e2e gate is packaged; full e2e PASS still requires validation=ok_full_e2e.

implementations.name: aioquic
implementations.next_action: Use as readable passive path-validation reference, not primary experiment
implementations.risk_note: Readable passive/path-validation reference; not a primary active-migration API candidate.

implementations.name: HAProxy QUIC
implementations.usage_reason: Popular proxy whose official docs currently state migration is unsupported
implementations.next_action: Use fresh local negative control as evidence that HTTP/3 proxy support does not imply active CM support
implementations.risk_note: HTTP/3 proxy success is a negative control for active migration support.

reporting_boundary.safe_claim: quic-go is not the only implementation with CM evidence; multiple non-quic-go stacks expose tests, runtime demos, path validation, or policy hooks.
reporting_boundary.unsafe_claim: All non-quic-go implementations provide equal active migration behavior, browser continuity, or production deployment success.
reporting_boundary.professor_answer: We used quic-go as the deepest controllable positive control, then added non-quic-go evidence as cross-implementation maturity, server/app demos, readiness blockers, and negative controls.
